content:
Our agent hallucinated a refund.

We had built a support bot on LangChain to handle order issues.
It worked in staging.
It failed on day 3 in production.

One user asked for a partial refund on a $48 order.
The agent replied with confidence, approved $120, and wrote a fake policy citation.
Our logs showed 17 similar attempts over the next hour.

We first blamed the model.
Then we tried a bigger model, tighter prompts, and a second verification step.

None of it fixed the core issue.

The real problem was simpler:
the agent had write access before it had proof.

It could read the order system, reason about the case, and act.
But there was no hard boundary between suggestion and execution.
One bad tool call was enough.

We changed one thing:
the agent could draft a refund request, but Stripe approvals moved behind a human gate.

The next week, refund errors dropped to zero.

My lesson from that outage:
AI agents are not broken because they think poorly.
They break when we let them act with more confidence than our systems can justify.

Where do you draw the line between agent autonomy and human approval?

Simple architecture beats clever architecture.

I learned that the hard way on a backend rewrite.

We had 14 services, gRPC between teams, and a beautiful diagram in Miro.
It looked like a senior engineer had painted it.

Then one small change took 11 days to ship.

The bottleneck wasn’t code.
It was coordination, versioning, and everyone being afraid to touch the seams.

We cut it down to 3 services and moved two workflows back into a single Rails app.
Not because microservices are bad.
Because our team was too small for the shape we’d built.

Deploys dropped from 6 steps to 1.
On-call pages fell by 40% in the next month.

The contrarian part:
architecture is not a trophy for maturity.
It’s a cost you pay every day.

The strongest argument for complexity is scale.
Sometimes that’s true.

But most teams I’ve seen don’t need more boundaries.
They need fewer places to fail.

What architecture choice looked smart on paper and slowed you down in practice?
